Community Initiatives Notice Of Motion And Motion To Stay Proceedings Pending Appeal
Off Calendar Apr-27-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances (by Zoom):
Allan Gomes, Esq.415-217-8802 Anderies & Gomes LLP for Plaintiff.
Wilson Lau, Esq. 415-353-0999 Bertrans FOx Elliot Osman & Wenzel for Defendant.
Laura Hurtado, Esq.415-938-1000 for Defendant Board of Trustees of the Leland Stanford.
David Winton, Esq. and William Zapf, Esq. 415-985-2111, Winton Strauss Law Group, P.C. for Defendant Community Initiatives. (in-person)

RULING - A R G U E D and Submitted. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT COMMUNITY INITIATIVES' Motion To Stay Proceedings Pending Appeal.

Community Initiatives' (CI) "motion to stay proceedings pending appeal" is denied.

By a January 23, 2023 order, this court disqualified Michael Vartain and his eponymous law firm from representing the University of San Francisco in this case. USF appealed that order; thus everyone seems to agree the order is automatically stayed. (URS Corp. v. Atkinson/Walsh Joint Venture (2017) 15 Cal.App.5th 872, 886-87.)

USF views the disqualification as collateral and wants to continue the case with new counsel to avoid delay. CI, on the other hand, seeks a discretionary stay of the entire action until USF's appeal is decided. While trial courts "have the power to issue discretionary stays" (Daly v. San Bernardino County Board of Supervisors (2021) 11 Cal.5th 1030, 1039), none is needed here.

CI argues that a stay "is warranted because Vartain refuses to refrain from continuing to represent, advise and assist USF adverse to" CI. (Memo. 7:19-21.) However, USF says its new counsel "will solely and completely handle all aspects of the trial court action." (Opp. 1:3-4.) Should CI learn otherwise, it may file a motion and the stay issue may be revisited.

Prevailing party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/RBU)

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Dec-22-2022 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances via Zoom Conference
Ross J. Vartain, Esq., VARTAIN LAW GROUP, P.C., [415] 391-1155, Attorneys for Plaintiff and Cross-Defendant UNIVERSITY OF SAN FRANCISCO,
William Zapf, Esq, KAISERDILLON PLLC, (202) 640-2850, wzapf@kaiserdillon.com, Attorneys for Defendant COMMUNITY INITIATIVES,
David C. Winton, Esq.., WINTON STRAUSS LAW GROUP, P.C., (415) 985-2111, dcw@wintonstrauss.com
Attorneys for Defendant COMMUNITY INITIATIVES,

Hearing is held. ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT COMMUNITY INITIATIVES' DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT.

Defendant Community Initiatives' demurrer to the first, second, third, and fourth causes of action of the First Amended Complaint is overruled.

Defendant's request for judicial notice of documents is denied. The existence and terms of a contract are not proper subjects of judicial notice when the opposing party objects. (Travelers Indemnity Co. of Connecticut v. Navigators Specialty Ins. Co. (2021) 70 Cal.App.5th 341, 354-355 & fn. 9 [trial court erred in sustaining demurrers to equitable contribution and equitable indemnity causes of action]; compare Chacon v. Union Pacific Railroad (2020) 56 Cal.App.5th 565, 572 [trial court did not err in taking judicial notice of settlement agreement and release where opposing party did not object to request for judicial notice].) Further, "a court cannot by means of judicial notice convert a demurrer into an incomplete evidentiary hearing in which the demurring party can present documentary evidence and the opposing party is bound by what that evidence appears to show." (Fremont Indemnity Co. v. Fremont General Corp. (2007) 148 Cal.App.4th 97, 114.) Defendant's reliance on unpublished opinions (Reply, 2 fn. 2, 3 fn. 3) is improper. (Cal. R. Ct. 8.1115(a).) Finally, Defendant has not authenticated the documents.

Plaintiff adequately pleads causes of action against Defendant for contractual and equitable indemnity, breach of contract, and declaratory relief. (See FAC 10, 13, 18-23, 47, 64-66.) Plaintiff may properly plead the legal effect of the underlying agreements, and is not required to plead evidentiary facts. As to the second cause of action for equitable indemnity, a plaintiff is permitted to plead alternative or inconsistent theories of recovery (Dubin v. Robert Newhall Chesebrough Trust (2002) 96 Cal.App.4th 465, 477.)

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schuman: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/EPS)
